title: Can You Get Progressive Contact Lenses? Multifocal Contacts Explained
description: Yes, progressive (multifocal) contact lenses exist. We explain how they work, who they're for, the pros and cons vs. progressive glasses, and what to expect.
tldr: Yes, multifocal contact lenses are the contact lens equivalent of progressive glasses. They correct both distance and near vision in a single lens using concentric ring or blended zone designs. They work well for many presbyopic patients but involve visual compromises — slightly softer vision at all distances compared to single vision contacts or progressive glasses.

definition.text: Contact lenses designed to correct presbyopia by incorporating multiple prescription powers within a single lens. They provide simultaneous correction for distance, intermediate, and near vision, eliminating the need for reading glasses or progressive eyeglasses. Also called progressive contact lenses or bifocal contacts.

sections.content:
Unlike progressive eyeglass lenses (where you look through different zones by moving your eyes), multifocal contacts present multiple powers to your eye simultaneously. Your brain learns to select the right focus for the task at hand.

Two main designs exist:

Concentric ring design: Alternating rings of distance and near power radiate outward from the lens centre. Some brands put distance in the centre, others put near in the centre. Your brain processes light from all rings simultaneously and suppresses the blurred image.

Blended/aspheric design: Power gradually changes from the centre to the periphery of the lens, similar to how progressive glasses work but in a circular pattern. This provides a more natural transition but typically delivers slightly less crisp vision at each distance.

This is one of the most common questions that comes up during eye exams, and the answer often depends on individual factors that only a professional assessment can evaluate. What works for one person may not be ideal for another — your prescription, daily activities, facial anatomy, and budget all play a role in determining the best approach.

If you're in the Edmonton area, Charm Optical offers comprehensive eye exams that go beyond just measuring your prescription. Their optometrists assess your overall eye health, discuss your visual needs, and recommend lens and frame options tailored to your lifestyle. With direct insurance billing for most Alberta providers and same-day glasses for many prescriptions, getting the right eyewear doesn't have to be complicated or expensive. Book online — appointments are usually available within a few days.

Multifocal contacts are designed for people with presbyopia — the age-related loss of near-focus ability that typically starts in the early to mid-40s. If you currently wear progressive glasses and want the freedom of contacts, multifocals are worth considering.

Good candidates tend to be:

  • Active people who find glasses inconvenient for sport, social events, or certain activities
  • People who dislike the look of progressive or reading glasses
  • Those with moderate presbyopia (ADD power up to about +2.50)
  • Current contact lens wearers who are starting to need reading glasses

They're less ideal for people who need very sharp near vision for detailed work (jewellers, surgeons), those with high astigmatism (multifocal toric options are limited), or anyone who struggles with contact lens handling.

sections.h2: The Visual Trade-Off: What to Expect
sections.content:
Here's the honest part that marketing glosses over: multifocal contacts involve visual compromises. Because your eye receives both distance and near light simultaneously, neither is quite as crisp as a single-focus solution.

Most wearers describe it as "good enough everywhere, perfect nowhere." Distance vision is slightly less sharp than with distance-only contacts. Near vision is functional but not as detailed as with dedicated reading glasses. Night driving may show halos around lights as the concentric rings create some light scatter.

Your brain adapts over 2–4 weeks. The initial impression often improves significantly as neural adaptation kicks in. Most successful wearers report being very satisfied after the adjustment period, even if the vision isn't technically as crisp as their glasses.

An eye exam with a thorough discussion of your visual priorities helps set realistic expectations. Book at Charm Optical to discuss whether multifocal contacts suit your specific needs.

Advantages of multifocal contacts:

  • No frames blocking peripheral vision
  • No head tilting to find the right zone (a common progressive glasses complaint)
  • Better for active lifestyles and sport
  • Aesthetic preference — some people simply prefer how they look without glasses

Advantages of progressive glasses:

  • Sharper vision at each distance — no simultaneous focus compromise
  • No touching your eyes or lens maintenance
  • No dry eye issues from contact wear
  • Generally more comfortable for all-day screen work
  • Lower annual cost ($300–$600 every 2 years vs. $400–$800 per year for contacts)
  • Available in far more prescription combinations

Many presbyopic people use both: multifocal contacts for active days, social events, and sport, with progressive glasses for work days and all-day comfort. Your insurance may cover both types in the same benefit period.

When comparing options, consider your daily routine and visual priorities rather than just price. The "best" choice is the one that matches how you actually use your eyes throughout the day. Someone who works on a computer for 8 hours has very different needs than someone who primarily drives or reads. An honest conversation with your optometrist about your daily visual demands will point you toward the right option far more effectively than online reviews.

Keep in mind that you're not locked into one choice forever. Many people own multiple pairs for different situations — dedicated computer glasses for the office, progressives for daily wear, and prescription sunglasses for driving. Charm Optical carries 500+ frames across every style and price point, making it easy to build a collection that covers all your visual needs. Their opticians can advise which lens types pair best with each frame style. Same-day glasses are available for many single vision prescriptions.

The fitting process for multifocal contacts is more involved than standard contacts. Your optometrist will:

  1. Assess your prescription, including the ADD power for near vision
  2. Evaluate your tear film and corneal health for contact lens suitability
  3. Select an initial trial lens brand and design based on your prescription and visual priorities
  4. Have you wear the trials for a week or two to evaluate real-world performance
  5. Adjust the lens parameters based on your feedback — this may take 2–3 fitting visits to optimize

Don't expect perfection from the first trial. Multifocal fitting is an iterative process, and patient feedback is essential. Tell your optometrist specifically what's working and what's not — "distance is good but I can't read my phone" is more useful than "they're okay."

Popular multifocal contact brands in Canada include Alcon Dailies Total1 Multifocal, CooperVision Biofinity Multifocal, and Johnson & Johnson Acuvue Oasys Multifocal. Your optometrist recommends based on your specific prescription and corneal characteristics.

Your eyes are one of the most important tools you have, and they deserve proper care and correction. The technology behind modern eyewear has advanced remarkably in recent years — from digital freeform progressive lenses that customize the optics to your exact wearing position, to lightweight titanium frames that weigh less than a coin, to anti-reflective coatings that virtually eliminate glare. Understanding these options empowers you to make better choices about your eye health and get glasses that genuinely improve your quality of life rather than just meeting a basic need.

Regular eye exams are the foundation of good vision care. The Canadian Association of Optometrists recommends exams every one to two years for adults and annually for children, seniors over 65, and anyone with diabetes or a family history of eye disease. A comprehensive exam does far more than measure your prescription — your optometrist checks the health of your retina, optic nerve, and blood vessels, catching conditions like glaucoma and macular degeneration in their earliest stages. Many serious eye conditions have no noticeable symptoms until significant damage has occurred, making preventive exams essential.

The best next step is always a professional eye exam. Your optometrist can evaluate your specific needs and recommend the right combination of lenses, coatings, and frames for your lifestyle and budget. In Alberta, most employer insurance plans cover comprehensive eye exams, and children under 18 and adults over 65 receive coverage through Alberta Health.

Charm Optical at 5035 Ellerslie Rd SW in South Edmonton offers thorough eye exams with experienced optometrists, a selection of over 500 designer frames from brands like Ray-Ban, Oakley, and Versace, and same-day glasses for many prescription types. They offer direct insurance billing for most Alberta providers, so you only pay the difference at the counter.

Whether you are buying your first pair of glasses, upgrading to progressive lenses, or just need an adjustment on your current frames, having a trusted local optician makes the process smoother. Book an appointment online — most appointments are available within a few days, and walk-ins are welcome for adjustments and frame browsing.

faqs.q: Are progressive contacts the same as multifocal contacts?
faqs.a: In practice, yes. 'Multifocal' is the technically correct term for contact lenses. 'Progressive' technically refers to the specific graduated lens design used in glasses. But many people use the terms interchangeably, and your optometrist will know what you mean.

faqs.q: How much do multifocal contacts cost in Canada?
faqs.a: About $400–$800 CAD per year, depending on brand, wearing schedule (daily disposable vs. monthly), and prescription complexity. Daily disposables are most convenient but cost more. Most insurance plans cover contact lenses or glasses — sometimes both.

faqs.q: Can you wear multifocal contacts if you have astigmatism?
faqs.a: Options exist but are limited. Multifocal toric contacts are available from some brands, but the combination of multifocal correction and astigmatism correction in one lens involves additional compromises. Your optometrist can assess whether available options would work for your specific Rx.

faqs.q: Do I need an eye exam for multifocal contacts?
faqs.a: Yes. A contact lens fitting is separate from a standard eye exam and glasses prescription. Contact lenses sit directly on your cornea and require additional measurements (corneal curvature, tear film assessment, lens fit evaluation). Book a comprehensive exam that includes a contact lens assessment.

faqs.q: What lens material is best for strong prescriptions?
faqs.a: High-index lenses (1.67 or 1.74) are best for strong prescriptions because they bend light more efficiently, resulting in thinner and lighter lenses. Standard plastic (CR-39) works well for mild prescriptions. Your optician at Charm Optical can recommend the ideal index based on your prescription strength.

faqs.q: How often should I replace my lenses?
faqs.a: Most optometrists recommend updating your prescription every 1-2 years. Lens coatings (anti-reflective, scratch-resistant) degrade over time even if your prescription hasn't changed. If you notice scratches, haze, or peeling, it's time for new lenses. Book an eye exam at Charm Optical to check your current prescription.
